verdict_reasoning: CRISP1 is a secreted cysteine-rich protein expressed in the epididymis and associated with sperm-egg interaction during fertilization. The protein is secreted into the epididymal lumen and binds to sperm. While the baseline biology describes it as secreted (soluble pool), CRISP proteins are known to associate with sperm plasma membranes and to have surface-interactive roles in gamete recognition and fusion. The protein has documented surface association on sperm cells during fertilization, a tissue-restricted (germline/reproductive) and developmental-stage-restricted context. This qualifies as contextual surface accessibility via tissue-restricted display and gamete-specific biology, not baseline somatic-cell surface residence.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Ambiguity between whether CRISP1 acts as a soluble ligand binding surface partners (secreted_only) versus having direct stable surface association on sperm membranes (contextual / tissue_restricted_surface). The NCBI summary emphasizes secretion into epididymal lumen and binding, but does not explicitly exclude membrane association on sperm during gamete interaction. CRISP-family proteins are known to have membrane-associated and zona-pellucida-interactive roles in fertilization, suggesting surface engagement in the reproductive context.
